speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, when I came to [? Sharkei, ?] I was advancing on the main road. And suddenly, I notice-- distant, maybe 500 meters-- a Jordanian tank [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] aiming towards us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sure enough, he started to fire on each truck. Big part of it was that the trucks were so close, he had no problem. One after the other was put out of order. And we lost there about 50 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my truck was hit right in the engine and hit my legs. And I was wounded. There remains one truck managed to make a u-turn. And whoever remained managed to jump into the truck and get back to the base. After that action, Givati decided to pull me out of service, not be anymore in the infantry fighting units. And they transferred me to, to the navy, which I became commander of the camp Bat Galim, biggest navy base in Haifa [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till, you know, my time came up. And I left the, the navy. And this was the end of 1949. So it's, you know, close. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I came out and I told you before, I tried to help my friends, fellow, you know, army people, to get settled. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And being the commander of the camp, there was coincidentally, a-- one of the soldiers who came from Portugal who was in concentration camp. One night, he was on duty and then became wild. And he opened that machine gun and started shoot at everybody. He was court marshal. And he got jail sentence many years. But I worked very hard to get him out of jail. And I succeeded. He had a trial, you know. And that's it. This was my-- the end of my army career. I was one of the 10 candidates was supposed to get top award medals in Israel. I didn't get it, because the, the government and the army had decided to give those medals to the families who lost their sons. And I didn't object, you know-- was happy they-- that they got it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was never afraid to go to any action, for the worse and the better. I always thought [INAUDIBLE] and a bullet has an address. I go out and have to be-- I would never worry. But I did it from my heart, and-- which I took a part of establishing Israel as a country in very decisive battles. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And when I finished, I decided to make army a career. And the navy had decided to send me to France to a military school in-- but the same time, I ask them for a-- two weeks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to go to United States to visit my family, which I never went to the military school. And I remained with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you remain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my parents were very unhappy, to an extent that my mother was crying daily. And same time, I got a, a job to work in the Israeli consulate, which I work for a year and a half. And I was recommended from Israel that I can work there in security. I worked with [INAUDIBLE], used to be his private security, plus other things, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed here. And I met my wife and married in 1953. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I have three children-- lovely children-- six grandchildren, which they're probably here now. And I'm very thankful that I'm alive. And, and I'm a success in my life and my business and all around. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I appreciate you gave me this opportunity. Because I never had that opportunity, you know, to express myself, which I never wanted to. But I, I feel good that I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you for doing it. And I'd just like to ask you, before we stop talking now and go to meeting your family and seeing the photographs, if there's anything that you'd like to add maybe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel it's a, a very important assignment that you people undertook. And I'm sure my children and my grandchildren, my great grandchildren, when they'll watch it, will be very important. They should see what I went through in my life and to remember me and to remember the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sort of.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very grateful that the whole family is together, except my youngest son, David. And I would like to introduce the family. To my left is my lovely wife, Anne, to my right, my oldest son, Mark, to my left, my daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon. [LAUGHTER] And as I mentioned, my youngest son, which is not present, David. Now in back of me is my dear daughter-in-law,

speaker: interviewee
text: Doreen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Doreen. [LAUGHTER In back of me is my dear son-in-law, Gideon. And I, I will introduce my grandchildren, Mark's grandchildren--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark's children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark's children is Adam in back of me, Stephie, sitting Mark's left, and Rachel, sitting on my lap.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren of Sharon is Monica in back of her mommy, Jeremy, sitting [INAUDIBLE] Anne. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And this pretty girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beautiful little girl in the red dress, Monica, which is very tired.

speaker: interviewee
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rebecca, Rebecca.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's behind you? [INTERPOSING VOICES] Oh, they already got you? Oh, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I really am very thankful that the whole family came, except David.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who couldn't make it. And I see everybody is happy that he came and be a part of me, of really a very important stories of my past life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, I thanked the crew, which were very great. And I thank very much the-- Mr. Spielberg-- right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is the organizer and contributor to this very important undertaking. Thank you. And I would really like that my wife could say, if you would.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we also-- and most of all, we thank God for having reached this stage, having made it possible for this marvelous feat of survival against great odds that Dov and his, as I have explained to you, his whole family, is an amazing story-- how 15 people who lived together with the German Wehrmacht in their home somehow were able, through wit and luck, of course, to survive as an intact family. It's a, it's a remarkable story. And of course, you know, my husband subsequently had many close brushes and near misses and, thank God, was able to survive. And we have reached this stage where we have joy in [HEBREW SPEECH], you know, from all our children and our grandchildren. And came here [HEBREW SPEECH], as they say in Hebrew-- let it go on with God's help. And we're very proud of our children. We're very, very proud of our grandchildren. My mother used to say that she had one son who went to yeshiva and had a good Jewish education. And she had seven grandchildren that did. And we're glad that we have six now that are continuing with the Jewish tradition. And may it so continue. We're very proud of them. And, and very-- thank God-- very happy. And I think it would be nice if they would share our thoughts. Maybe the grandchildren and the children--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Mark, want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I'm on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just wanted to say that I'm also, you know, certainly very proud of my father and his, and his accomplishing up to this point so much in his life, really having taught me many, many wonderful things in my life. Just certain things come to mind, such as the excellent work ethic that I've-- I'd like to think that I've continued, also, very importantly, the tremendous, tremendously strong feeling of Israel, the belief in the state of Israel, and just, just overall just very, very proud to be here and be-- to be part of this gathering. Sharon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sharon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, I'm very proud of the, the history of my father, how he was able to overcome such odds to be able to survive. And then his trip to Israel, the fact that he came so far in the Israeli Army as well-- I wonder if he'd stayed there, what his position would be today. And a lot of my father's friends became generals in the Israeli army. And he knew all these players that are very high up on the scene today. And I'm sure if he had stayed in Israel and had continued serving in the army, that today, he would have been up there with all these--

speaker: interviewee
text: Luminaries.

speaker: interviewee
text: Luminaries, right-- like this week, Rabin was assassinated, which is a very important, very sad occasion. And my father knew Rabin. He worked-- he served under him. And he had contact with him and with many other important people in the Israeli, in Israeli history. And it's just wonderful that here he came from this background living in Poland and having his family split up as it had been, and then going to Israel almost as an orphan, even though he wasn't-- that he was able to have the strength to be able to succeed so much in the-- in Israel after having had such a difficult beginning. And then coming to America and once again starting anew, with my mother at his side, to, to build a beautiful business and to have a, a wonderful life here, and yet have that strong connection still with Israel. My parents go very often to Israel. And their, their closest friends, my father's closest friends, are all there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's just he's, he's come a long way. And he's done wonderful things. And it's a wonderful example for my children, for the grandchildren, and future generations, to see how much he's accomplished.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're very proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would any grandchildren like to, to share a few words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam, would you like to say something about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Doreen? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gideon? Gideon will-- GIDEON (SON-IN-LAW): I'll add a word, or two words. First of all, I think that very often when you hear people talk about things that happened in the early part of their life, you tend to think that they're exaggerating it, for better or worse. And I know that very often when my father-in-law and his brothers and sisters spoke about their life experiences, everything that happened to them pre-war was in a very grand state.

speaker: interviewer
text: And several years ago, when my in-laws had the opportunity to go back and visit in Pruchnik, my mother-in-law came back and reported that everything they said is true. And the thing that stands out in my mind was that when they came to the house, it truly was the largest house in the town. And it still stands today. I understand it functions as a government building. Or a post office, is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Post office and nursery school. GIDEON (SON-IN-LAW): And I think that we just have to believe and understand that the memories or-- you know, they're very real memories, for better and worse. And I think that seeing the house standing as it did was a, was a reality check. It was very objective. It really was as this young boy remembered it. And the other thing I would say-- changing positions here-- is that I think for my father-in-law and his siblings, they're really not survivors. I think that when they came here, somehow or another they managed to turn the page. I think they're livers. I think they really conducted themselves with full gusto. And I don't know why I'm getting so emotional. I'm very unconnected to it. But I, I think it's extremely admirable. And I don't think of them as survivors. I really think that each of them replanted themselves and carried on in a very full, almost unblemished way.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: Monica you want to say something? MONICA (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): I just want to thank my in-laws for raising such a nice son. And I'm very, very happy that we're able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hear, hear. MONICA (DAUGHTER-IN-LAW): Give them this [HEBREW SPEECH] and to be able to raise our children in the way that they set, set for, for us with Jewish education. And I'm just very happy. And we hope that we always make them happy and proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you do. And now we're on to the next generation, who does us proud. We are coming to, very shortly in another few months, a great milestone, the bar mitzfah, our first--

speaker: interviewee
text: First Bar Mitzfah in our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandson. And we're looking forward to this, [HEBREW SPEECH]. God willing.

speaker: interviewer
text: GIDEON (SON-IN-LAW): Why don't we finish with a song? Maybe the kids will just sing something.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want to say a few words, Monica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Monica, say something. Come on. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do want to sing a song? Do you have a song you can sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this guy wanted to say something, didn't you? You were really looking forward to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Rachel Feit. Bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you? What do you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jeremy Barry Feit.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Say what you think about Saba Dov. First of all, what do the grandchildren like to do? And all the kids? What do you like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Loud.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephanie wants to say something. Go ahead, Steph. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephanie, come on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Come on, Steph, what you want to say? Don't be shy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead. Say something. The camera is on you. Say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: You wanted to say-- say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very happy my daddy was [INAUDIBLE] and my mommy [INAUDIBLE]. Bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what do are children do with Saba Dov? What do you do? What do you like to touch?

speaker: interviewee
text: We like to touch his mustache. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's the thing that everywhere does, even Rebecca.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of my grandfather, that he raised such good children and that, that he raised such great children to be such great parents to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's lovely. That's great. And would, would everyone like to sing a song? What? OK, Stephanie, what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of my whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Monica wants to say some words too.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am, too. I'm very proud of my-- all of my family. And I'm glad they were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. And what, Monica? MONICA (GRANDDAUGHTER): I'm very proud that my grandfather survived. Otherwise, my mother and my uncle and my other uncle and I wouldn't be here. That's true. That's true. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't either.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Something funny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something about Saba Dov being a great fighter? How about that? You learned-- what did you learn from them that way? What did you learn? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: How does he play with you?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Does anyone want to say a closing word, or are we all set?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd like to sing something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to sing a song?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Sing it then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very happy that my, my grandpa survived through the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good. And now let's see what song we're going to sing quick?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Hatikvah?'

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Hatikvah.' That's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, one, two, three. [SINGING - 'HATIKVAH']

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family section, I think [INAUDIBLE]. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Begin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe that picture was taking in 1931. And in the picture is my youngest brother, Eli, myself, my older brother, Israel, and then Joseph. To the right is myself. The younger one, which is next to me on the bottom, is my younger brother, Eli. Next to me in the middle is Israel. And to my left is Joe. Just pictures of my family-- to my right is my sister, Miriam. Next to Miriam is my cousin, Irving Feit. In the middle next to Irving Feit is my father. To the left to my father is my mother, Sara. And to the left of my mother is my cousin, Fanny, which is the wife of Irving Feit. And that picture was taking in Brooklyn, in the United States, in 1949, in the house where my parents lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- my graduation and the picture of my friends. And I would say more than 50% of those students were killed during the independence of the state of Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have great memories to that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're pictured where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture that was taking in Mikveh Israel, which I-- when I came to Israel, I joined that school, which I spent three years in there, which I graduated. And I graduated in 1945. This is my picture, taking in the Navy in 1949, which I had joined the navy after I was wounded and transferred to the navy. But this is a, a picture of when I joined the, the Israeli army from the Palmach. And this is my identification card being in the army. This picture was taking in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taking in 1947 when I joined the army. And we are heading towards the Negev. This picture was taken in 1949, which I was transferred from the army to the navy after I was wounded. And I became their commander of Camp Bat Galim and also [INAUDIBLE]. This picture was taking in beginning of 1942, , when I arrived in Israel. And I was welcome by the leader of the Ihud in Israel, Henrietta Szold. And I am in this picture, the fourth one from the right. OK. This picture was taken in 1948, the very, very crucial time in Israel. We were assigned to a certain area, which was very secure. And I got a call from the headquarters that, that the photographers from the are coming. And they will interview us and taking picture. And this picture was on the first page in I am in the center in black clothes. And the, the other four people never survived. They got killed during the action. OK. This picture was taken in 1984. Here is my whole family-- brothers and sisters-- six brothers and three sisters. I am on top on the right side. Yeah. This picture is my son, David, who was taken during his graduation of law school at UCLA. Sorry that he couldn't make it tonight.
